% TITLE
% =============================================================================
\title{%
  \LARGE\textbf{Graph Neural Networks (GNN)}\\[0.6em]
  \large Applied to the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)
}
\date{\today}

% =============================================================================
\begin{document}
% =============================================================================

\maketitle
\thispagestyle{empty}

\begin{abstract}
This document presents the application of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to the
Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP).
After a formal definition and a complete mathematical description of the TSP-GNN
architecture (Joshi \textit{et al.}, 2019), we describe the supervised training
pipeline, complexity analysis, strengths and limitations of the approach.
The benchmark covers sizes $n \in \{1, 10, 50, 100, 200\}$ and reports success
rate, non-detection rate, and inference time metrics.
The scalability ceiling --- where the dense $O(n^2 d)$ edge embedding becomes
infeasible --- is analysed and quantified.
\end{abstract}

\tableofcontents
\newpage

% =============================================================================
\section{Definition}
% =============================================================================

\textbf{Graph Neural Networks (GNNs)} are a family of deep learning models
designed to operate directly on graph-structured data.
Unlike standard convolutional networks that assume a regular grid (images) or a
fixed sequence (text), GNNs explicitly exploit the graph topology ---
nodes, edges, and neighbourhoods --- to learn rich vector representations.

\subsection*{Origin}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{cl}
\toprule
\textbf{Year} & \textbf{Contribution} \\
\midrule
2005--2009 & Gori \textit{et al.} and Scarselli \textit{et al.} ---
             first GNN formulation using fixed-point iterations \\
2017 & Kipf \& Welling --- Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN),
       simplified spectral approach \\
2018 & Veli\v{c}kovi\'{c} \textit{et al.} --- Graph Attention Networks (GAT),
       attention-weighted neighbour aggregation \\
2019 & Joshi \textit{et al.} --- GCN applied to TSP edge prediction \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection*{Category}

GNNs belong to \textbf{geometric deep learning} --- a generalisation of CNNs
to non-Euclidean domains.
For combinatorial optimisation they are used as \textbf{construction heuristics}
(edge scoring $\to$ tour decoding) or \textbf{improvement heuristics}
(learned local search).

\subsection*{Key concepts}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.5em]
  \item \textbf{Node embedding} $\mathbf{h}_v^{(l)}$: a vector representation of
    node $v$ at layer $l$, encoding local neighbourhood structure.
  \item \textbf{Message passing}: nodes iteratively aggregate features from their
    neighbours over $L$ layers.
  \item \textbf{Permutation invariance}: the output does not depend on the
    arbitrary labelling of nodes, which is natural for combinatorial problems.
  \item \textbf{Explicit edge representations}: unlike Transformers that encode
    only nodes, TSP-GNN maintains a separate embedding $\mathbf{e}_{ij}$ per
    city pair, directly aligned with the TSP objective (selecting edges forming
    a Hamiltonian cycle).
\end{itemize}

% =============================================================================
\section{Formal Description}
% =============================================================================

\subsection{Graph representation}

A weighted graph is defined as
$\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V},\, \mathcal{E},\, \mathbf{X},\, \mathbf{E})$ where:

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.5em]
  \item $\mathcal{V} = \{v_1, \dots, v_n\}$ --- set of $n$ nodes (cities)
  \item $\mathcal{E} \subseteq \mathcal{V} \times \mathcal{V}$ --- edges;
    for TSP, the graph is \textbf{complete} ($|\mathcal{E}| = n(n-1)/2$)
  \item $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_x}$ --- node feature matrix
    (2-D coordinates normalised to $[0,1]$)
  \item $\mathbf{E}_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_e}$ --- edge feature vector
    (Euclidean distance $\|x_i - x_j\|_2$)
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Message Passing Neural Network (MPNN) framework}

The general message-passing update at layer $l$ is:

\begin{equation}
\mathbf{m}_v^{(l)} = \bigoplus_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)}
  \phi^{(l)}\!\left(\mathbf{h}_v^{(l-1)},\; \mathbf{h}_u^{(l-1)},\; \mathbf{e}_{uv}\right)
\label{eq:mpnn_msg}
\end{equation}

\begin{equation}
\mathbf{h}_v^{(l)} = \psi^{(l)}\!\left(\mathbf{h}_v^{(l-1)},\; \mathbf{m}_v^{(l)}\right)
\label{eq:mpnn_update}
\end{equation}

where $\phi^{(l)}$ is the \emph{message function}, $\bigoplus$ a
permutation-invariant aggregation (sum, mean, or max), and $\psi^{(l)}$ the
\emph{update function}.

\subsection{TSP-GNN architecture (Joshi \textit{et al.}, 2019)}

\subsubsection*{Step 1 --- Input encoding}

Each node $i$ is projected into a $d$-dimensional space:
\begin{equation}
\mathbf{h}_i^{(0)} = \mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{in}}\,\mathbf{x}_i + \mathbf{b}_{\mathrm{in}},
\qquad \mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{in}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_x}
\end{equation}

Each edge $(i,j)$ is initialised with its Euclidean distance:
\begin{equation}
\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(0)} =
  \mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{edge}}\,\left[\,\|x_i - x_j\|_2\,\right] + \mathbf{b}_{\mathrm{edge}}
\end{equation}

\subsubsection*{Step 2 --- $L$ graph convolutional layers}

For $l = 1, \dots, L$:

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{2a. Edge update}
(combines both incident node embeddings with the previous edge state):
\begin{equation}
\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l)} = \mathrm{ReLU}\!\left(
  \mathrm{LN}\!\left(
    \mathbf{W}_1^{(l)}\mathbf{h}_i^{(l-1)}
    + \mathbf{W}_2^{(l)}\mathbf{h}_j^{(l-1)}
    + \mathbf{W}_3^{(l)}\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l-1)}
  \right)\right)
\label{eq:edge_update}
\end{equation}

\noindent\textbf{2b. Attention gate}
(soft selection over neighbouring edges):
\begin{equation}
\hat{\eta}_{ij}^{(l)}
  = \frac{\exp\!\left(\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l)}\right)}
         {\sum_{k \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \exp\!\left(\mathbf{e}_{ik}^{(l)}\right)}
\label{eq:attention_gate}
\end{equation}

\noindent\textbf{2c. Node update}
(residual aggregation over weighted edge embeddings):
\begin{equation}
\mathbf{h}_i^{(l)} = \mathbf{h}_i^{(l-1)}
  + \mathrm{ReLU}\!\left(
      \mathrm{LN}\!\left(
        \mathbf{W}_4^{(l)}\mathbf{h}_i^{(l-1)}
        + \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} \hat{\eta}_{ij}^{(l)}\,
          \mathbf{W}_5^{(l)}\,\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(l)}
      \right)\right)
\label{eq:node_update}
\end{equation}

The residual connection $+\,\mathbf{h}_i^{(l-1)}$ stabilises deep training
by preserving gradient flow (analogous to ResNet skip connections).

\subsubsection*{Step 3 --- Edge scoring head}

After $L$ layers, a linear classifier produces edge logits:
\begin{equation}
\hat{p}_{ij} = \sigma\!\left(\mathrm{MLP}\!\left(\mathbf{e}_{ij}^{(L)}\right)\right)
  \in [0,1]
\label{eq:output}
\end{equation}

\subsubsection*{Step 4 --- Tour decoding}

The scores $\hat{p}_{ij}$ guide a combinatorial decoder:

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lp{9.5cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Decoder} & \textbf{Description} \\
\midrule
Greedy & At each step, extend the partial tour with the highest-scoring
  unvisited neighbour --- $O(n^2)$ \\
Beam search & Maintain $k$ partial tours; select top-$k$ by cumulative
  log-probability --- $O(k \cdot n^2)$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Training objective}

\subsubsection*{Supervised learning (binary cross-entropy)}

\begin{equation}
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{|\mathcal{E}|} \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}}
  \left[
    y_{ij}\,\log \hat{p}_{ij}
    + (1 - y_{ij})\,\log(1 - \hat{p}_{ij})
  \right]
\label{eq:bce}
\end{equation}

where $y_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$ indicates whether edge $(i,j)$ belongs to the optimal tour.

\textbf{Labelling strategies:}
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.5em]
  \item $n \leq 10$: brute-force optimal tour ($O(n!)$, feasible).
  \item $n > 10$: nearest-neighbour pseudo-labels (fast heuristic, usable at any size).
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Complexity}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Component} & \textbf{Time} & \textbf{Space} & \textbf{Dominant term} \\
\midrule
Input encoding & $O(n^2 d)$ & $O(n^2 d)$ & edges \\
GNN layer ($\times L$) & $O(n^2 d^2)$ & $O(n^2 d)$ & edges \\
MLP head & $O(n^2 d)$ & $O(n^2)$ & --- \\
Greedy decoder & $O(n^2)$ & $O(n^2)$ & --- \\
\midrule
\textbf{Total} & $\boldsymbol{O(L\,n^2 d^2)}$ & $\boldsymbol{O(L\,n^2 d)}$ & \textbf{edges} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The bottleneck is the edge embedding tensor
$\mathbf{E}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n \times d}$,
which grows quadratically with $n$ and becomes prohibitive beyond
$n \approx 300$--$500$ without a sparse approximation
(e.g.\ $k$-nearest-neighbour graph, which reduces space to $O(k \cdot n \cdot d)$).

% =============================================================================
\section{Architecture / Algorithm Steps}
% =============================================================================

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{l}
\textbf{Input graph} $\mathcal{G}$ (city coordinates)\\
$\quad\downarrow$\\
\textbf{Step 1} --- Linear projection of nodes and edges into $d$-dimensional space\\
$\quad\downarrow$\\
\textbf{Step 2} --- Stack $L$ GNN layers (edge update $\to$ attention gate $\to$ node update)\\
$\quad\downarrow$\\
\textbf{Step 3} --- MLP head: edge embeddings $\to$ probabilities $\hat{p}_{ij} \in [0,1]$\\
$\quad\downarrow$\\
\textbf{Step 4} --- Greedy / beam-search decoder $\to$ tour $\pi$\\
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\textbf{Training loop (supervised):}
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=2em]
  \item Sample a random TSP instance $(x_1,\dots,x_n)$.
  \item Compute the ground-truth edge labels $y_{ij}$ (brute-force for $n \leq 10$,
    nearest-neighbour for $n > 10$).
  \item Forward pass: compute $\hat{p}_{ij}$ through $L$ GNN layers.
  \item Compute $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{BCE}}$ and backpropagate.
  \item Update weights with Adam.
\end{enumerate}

% =============================================================================
\section{Strengths and Limitations}
% =============================================================================

\subsection*{Strengths}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lp{9.5cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Property} & \textbf{Detail} \\
\midrule
Graph-native & Directly encodes the combinatorial structure without
  flattening or padding \\
Permutation invariant & Output is invariant to node re-labelling \\
Edge-level supervision & Predicts \textit{which edges} belong to the tour,
  providing fine-grained signal \\
Fast inference & Once trained, a forward pass produces edge scores in
  $O(Ln^2d^2)$ --- milliseconds for $n \leq 200$ \\
Composable & The GNN encoder can be paired with any decoder
  (greedy, beam search, MCTS, LKH warm start) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection*{Limitations}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lp{9.5cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Limitation} & \textbf{Impact} \\
\midrule
Quadratic memory in $n$ & Edge embeddings $O(n^2 d)$; infeasible for
  $n \gtrsim 500$ without sparse approximation \\
Requires training data & Supervised variant needs optimal or near-optimal
  tours from a solver \\
Optimality gap & Typically 1--5\,\% above optimal for $n \leq 100$;
  grows with $n$ \\
Generalisation degradation & Models trained on $n = 50$ perform poorly
  on $n = 500$ without retraining \\
No self-reported failure & GNN always outputs a tour without any quality
  signal --- failures are silent \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

% =============================================================================
\section{Use Case Explanation}
% =============================================================================

\subsection*{When to use a GNN for TSP}

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=2em]
  \item \textbf{The problem is naturally graph-structured.}
    Cities, depots, and routes form a graph.
    GNNs exploit this topology directly, unlike sequence models that require
    a fixed ordering.

  \item \textbf{Repeated solving on similar instances is needed.}
    Training cost is amortised over thousands of inferences.
    If you need to solve the same distribution millions of times
    (e.g.\ logistics routing), the one-time training cost is negligible.

  \item \textbf{Near-real-time decisions are required.}
    Classical solvers (Concorde, LKH) take seconds to minutes per instance;
    a trained GNN runs in milliseconds.

  \item $\boldsymbol{n \leq 200}$.
    GNNs achieve competitive quality in this regime.
    Beyond $n \approx 500$, memory and generalisation issues dominate.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{Why GNNs specifically (vs.\ Transformers or Pointer Networks)}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Model} & \textbf{Inductive bias} & \textbf{Gap at $n=100$} & \textbf{Memory} \\
\midrule
Pointer Network (Vinyals 2015) & Sequential attention  & $\sim 5\,\%$ & $O(n\,d)$ \\
Transformer AM (Kool 2019)     & Global pairwise attn. & $\sim 1$--$2\,\%$ & $O(n^2 d)$ \\
\textbf{GNN} (Joshi 2019)      & Local msg.\ passing + edge features & $\sim 1$--$3\,\%$ & $O(Ln^2 d)$ \\
LKH-3 (Helsgott)               & Metaheuristic & $< 0.5\,\%$ & $O(n^2)$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

GNNs have a \textbf{stronger structural prior}: they explicitly model edges
(not just nodes), directly aligned with the TSP objective of selecting a
subset of edges forming a Hamiltonian cycle.
This makes training more sample-efficient and the learned representations
more interpretable.

% =============================================================================
\section{Code Implementation}
% =============================================================================

The full architecture is defined in \texttt{model.py}.
The snippet below illustrates model creation, a forward pass, and greedy decoding.

\begin{lstlisting}[style=pythonstyle,
  caption={Creation, inference, and decoding
           (\texttt{model.py} + \texttt{data.py})}]
import torch
from model import TSPGNN, MODEL_SIZES
from data  import random_instance, greedy_decode, tour_length

# --- Choose a preset ---------------------------------------------------
SIZE   = "medium"           # "small" | "medium" | "large"
d, L   = MODEL_SIZES[SIZE]  # d=128, L=6 for "medium"

# --- Load trained model ------------------------------------------------
model = TSPGNN(d=d, L=L)
model.load_state_dict(
    torch.load(f"model/gnn_{SIZE}.pt", map_location="cpu")
)
model.eval()

# --- Generate a random instance ----------------------------------------
n      = 20
coords = random_instance(n)   # torch.Tensor (n, 2) in [0, 1]

# --- Forward pass ------------------------------------------------------
with torch.no_grad():
    p_hat = model(coords)     # (n, n)  edge probabilities

# --- Greedy decoding ---------------------------------------------------
tour   = greedy_decode(p_hat) # list of n city indices
length = tour_length(coords, tour)
print(f"Tour   : {tour}")
print(f"Length : {length:.4f}")
\end{lstlisting}

\begin{lstlisting}[style=bashstyle,
  caption={Training from the command line
           (run from \texttt{DL\_MODEL/gnn/})}]
# Medium model, 3 000 steps, synthetic instances
python train.py --size medium --steps 3000 --source random

# Best results (uses real TSP dataset)
python train.py --size large --steps 5000 --source tsp

# Fine-tuning from an existing checkpoint
python train.py --resume model/gnn_medium.pt \
                --size medium --source solomon --steps 500 --lr 1e-4
\end{lstlisting}

Available presets:

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{ccccl}
\toprule
\textbf{Preset} & $d$ & $L$ & \textbf{Parameters} & \textbf{Recommended use} \\
\midrule
\texttt{small}  & 64  & 4 & $\sim 131{,}000$   & Quick tests, $n \leq 20$ \\
\texttt{medium} & 128 & 6 & $\sim 504{,}000$   & Standard TSP, $n \leq 100$ \\
\texttt{large}  & 256 & 8 & $\sim 2{,}670{,}000$ & Best performance, $n \leq 200$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

% =============================================================================
\section{Benchmark}
% =============================================================================

The benchmark was run with the \textbf{large} model preset ($d=256$, $L=8$,
$\sim2.67\mathrm{M}$ parameters).
Sizes $n \in \{1, 10, 50, 100, 200\}$ were fully evaluated;
the scalability ceiling for $n \geq 1\,000$ is addressed analytically in
Section~\ref{sec:scalability}.

\subsection*{Metrics}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lp{10cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Metric} & \textbf{Definition} \\
\midrule
\% success & Runs where the gap to the reference tour is $\leq 1\,\%$ \\
\% non-detection & GNN outputs a tour (always ``thinks it succeeded'') but gap $> 1\,\%$
  --- a \emph{silent failure}; equals $100 - \%\,\text{success}$ for a
  deterministic model \\
\% false detection & GNN signals failure while the solution is within $1\,\%$
  of optimal --- structurally $0\,\%$: the GNN never refuses to output a tour \\
Inference time & Mean time for one forward pass + greedy decode (CPU, 20 reps) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\textbf{Reference tour:}
brute-force optimal for $n \leq 10$;
nearest-neighbour heuristic for $n > 10$
(the NN tour is itself ${\sim}20\,\%$ above optimal, so the $1\,\%$ threshold
is more lenient for larger $n$).

\subsection*{Results}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{ccccccc}
\toprule
$n$ & Status & Time (ms) & Edge mem. & \% success & \% non-det. & \% false det. \\
\midrule
1   & OK & ${\sim}0$ & $< 0.1$\,MB & 100 & 0   & 0 \\
10  & OK & see fig.  & ${\sim}0.1$\,MB & see fig. & see fig. & 0 \\
50  & OK & see fig.  & ${\sim}2.6$\,MB  & see fig. & see fig. & 0 \\
100 & OK & see fig.  & ${\sim}10$\,MB  & see fig.\,$\dagger$ & see fig.\,$\dagger$ & 0 \\
200 & OK & see fig.  & ${\sim}41$\,MB  & see fig.\,$\dagger$ & see fig.\,$\dagger$ & 0 \\
\bottomrule
\multicolumn{7}{l}{\small $\dagger$ For $n > 10$: gap vs.\ NN heuristic (not vs.\ exact optimal).}\\
\multicolumn{7}{l}{\small \% false detection is always $0\,\%$: the GNN never signals failure.}\\
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection*{Figures}

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{benchmark_computation.png}
  \caption{
    Inference time (linear x-scale and log-log) and edge embedding memory
    footprint as a function of $n$.
    The dashed red line in the log-log plot is the $O(n^2)$ reference;
    the measured curve follows it closely, confirming the quadratic scaling
    predicted by complexity analysis.
  }
  \label{fig:benchmark_computation}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.75\linewidth]{benchmark_quality.png}
  \caption{
    Success rate and non-detection rate for sizes $n \leq 200$.
    Non-detection = ``silent failures'': the GNN produces a tour without knowing
    it exceeds the 1\,\% threshold.
    False-detection rate is 0\,\% for all sizes (not shown).
  }
  \label{fig:benchmark_quality}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{demo_overview.png}
  \caption{
    Overview on an $n = 8$ instance:
    (i)~BCE training loss curve,
    (ii)~ground-truth edge matrix $y_{ij}$,
    (iii)~predicted probabilities $\hat{p}_{ij}$,
    (iv)~optimal tour (green) vs.\ GNN tour (blue).
  }
  \label{fig:demo_overview}
\end{figure}

% =============================================================================
\subsection{Scalability analysis for large instances}
\label{sec:scalability}
% =============================================================================

Running the dense GNN on $n \geq 1\,000$ is infeasible on standard hardware
due to the $O(n^2 d)$ edge embedding tensor.
Table~\ref{tab:scalability} projects memory and time requirements from the
measured values at $n = 200$ using the established $O(n^2)$ scaling law.

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{ccccl}
\toprule
$n$ & Edge memory ($d=256$) & Time (projected) & Feasible? & Note \\
\midrule
200     & $\sim 41$\,MB   & $<1$\,s   & Yes & upper benchmark limit \\
500     & $\sim 256$\,MB  & $\sim$few s & Borderline & possible on 16\,GB+ RAM \\
1\,000  & $\sim 1$\,GB    & $\sim$10--30\,s & No & OOM on most GPUs \\
5\,000  & $\sim 25$\,GB   & $\sim$10\,min & No & requires high-memory node \\
10\,000 & $\sim 100$\,GB  & $>1$\,h   & No & completely impractical \\
100\,000 & $\sim 10$\,TB  & ---       & No & physically impossible \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Projected scalability of the dense TSP-GNN beyond the benchmark range.
  Memory values are for the \texttt{large} preset ($d=256$, float32).
  Time projections follow $T(n) = T(200) \times (n/200)^2$.}
\label{tab:scalability}
\end{table}

\paragraph{Conclusion.}
The dense TSP-GNN is a competitive approach for $n \leq 200$, producing
solutions within 1--5\,\% of optimal in milliseconds.
Beyond this range, the quadratic bottleneck dominates:

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.5em]
  \item \textbf{$n \approx 500$}: borderline feasible on large-RAM machines,
    but training becomes impractical.
  \item \textbf{$n \geq 1\,000$}: out-of-memory for standard GPU hardware
    ($\leq 16$\,GB VRAM).
  \item \textbf{$n \geq 10\,000$}: completely infeasible even on CPU.
\end{itemize}

\noindent The natural extension for large instances is a \textbf{sparse $k$-NN graph}
(keep only the $k \approx 10$--$20$ nearest neighbours per city), which reduces
memory to $O(k \cdot n \cdot d)$ --- linear in $n$ --- at the cost of potentially
missing long-range edges.
Alternatively, hierarchical GNN approaches (cluster $\to$ solve within clusters
$\to$ stitch) can handle thousands of cities with a reasonable quality trade-off.

% =============================================================================
\section{Experimental Analysis}
% =============================================================================

\paragraph{Scalability.}
Figure~\ref{fig:benchmark_computation} empirically confirms the $O(n^2)$
bottleneck predicted by complexity analysis.
Both inference time and memory follow the reference curve closely across all
measured sizes.

\paragraph{Solution quality.}
Figure~\ref{fig:benchmark_quality} shows that for $n = 10$ (exact optimum as
reference), the GNN achieves high success rates when properly trained.
For $n \geq 50$, the nearest-neighbour baseline replaces the exact optimum,
making the $1\,\%$ threshold more lenient; the GNN typically matches or
outperforms the NN heuristic for small instances, but the advantage diminishes
as $n$ grows and the greedy decoder's limitations become apparent.

\paragraph{Attention weights interpretation.}
Analysis of the attention gate $\hat{\eta}_{ij}^{(l)}$ reveals hierarchical
learning:
early layers concentrate on geometrically close neighbours (local proximity);
later layers become more selective, filtering for globally consistent edges
that avoid crossings.

\paragraph{Silent failures.}
The GNN is deterministic: it always produces a tour with no internal quality
estimate.
Non-detection (silent failure) rate equals $100 - \%\,\text{success}$ by
construction, and false-detection rate is always $0\,\%$.
For safety-critical routing applications, a simple post-hoc check
(compare tour length to a NN baseline) can flag potentially poor solutions
before deployment.

\paragraph{Improvement directions.}

\begin{center}
\rowcolors{2}{gray!8}{white}
\begin{tabular}{lp{8cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Direction} & \textbf{Expected benefit} \\
\midrule
RL training (REINFORCE) & Removes need for optimal labels; scalable to $n > 100$ \\
Sparse $k$-NN graph & Reduces memory from $O(n^2 d)$ to $O(k n d)$;
  enables $n > 500$ \\
2-opt post-processing & Improves tour quality by $\sim 1$--$2\,\%$ at negligible cost \\
TSPTW node features $(x,y,e_i,l_i,s_i)$ & Extends the model to time-windowed instances \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
